title: Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)
description: Understanding the FDA's framework for regulating software that qualifies as a medical device.

learningObjectives.text: Define Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) and distinguish it from SiMD
Explain the IMDRF risk categorization framework for SaMD
Identify which healthcare AI products qualify as SaMD

contentSections.body:
### What Is SaMD?

The International Medical Device Regulators Forum (IMDRF) defines **Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)** as software intended to be used for one or more medical purposes that performs these purposes without being part of a hardware medical device.

Key distinctions:
- **SaMD (Software as a Medical Device):** Standalone software that is itself a medical device. Example: an app that analyzes retinal images to screen for diabetic retinopathy.
- **SiMD (Software in a Medical Device):** Software that is part of a hardware device. Example: firmware inside an MRI scanner that reconstructs images.
- **Not a medical device:** Software that supports administrative functions (scheduling, billing), general wellness apps (fitness tracking without diagnostic claims), and EHR software (unless it includes CDS features that qualify as SaMD).

The boundary between SaMD and non-device software is one of the most consequential regulatory questions in health AI. Making a diagnostic or treatment claim generally pushes software into SaMD territory. Providing general information or wellness guidance generally does not.

### The IMDRF Risk Framework

The IMDRF categorizes SaMD risk based on two dimensions:

**Significance of the information provided by the SaMD:**
- **Treat or diagnose:** Highest significance (e.g., AI that autonomously diagnoses diabetic retinopathy)
- **Drive clinical management:** Moderate significance (e.g., AI that prioritizes radiology worklist by urgency)
- **Inform clinical management:** Lower significance (e.g., AI that flags potential drug interactions for pharmacist review)

**Seriousness of the healthcare situation:**
- **Critical:** Life-threatening conditions (e.g., stroke, sepsis)
- **Serious:** Conditions requiring intervention (e.g., cancer detection)
- **Non-serious:** Conditions with minimal immediate risk

The combination of these two dimensions produces a risk category from I (lowest) to IV (highest). Category IV — software that treats or diagnoses critical conditions — faces the most stringent regulatory requirements.

### The FDA's Evolving Approach

The FDA has authorized over 950 AI/ML-enabled medical devices as of early 2025. The agency has developed specific guidance for AI/ML-based SaMD, including the concept of a **Predetermined Change Control Plan** — allowing manufacturers to pre-specify how their AI model will be updated after approval without requiring a new submission for each update.

**Career tip:** Understanding SaMD classification is essential for roles at health AI companies, regulatory consulting firms, and health system innovation offices. The question 'is this product SaMD?' determines whether a project needs a regulatory submission, which pathway to use, and how much clinical evidence is required.

title: FDA Regulatory Pathways for AI/ML Devices
description: 510(k), De Novo, and PMA: choosing the right pathway for clinical AI products.

learningObjectives.text: Describe the three main FDA regulatory pathways for medical devices
Explain how predicate devices work in the 510(k) pathway
Determine which pathway is appropriate for a given clinical AI product

contentSections.body:
### The Three Pathways

**510(k) Clearance** — 'Substantially equivalent to a predicate'
- Used when a legally marketed device (the predicate) already exists with the same intended use and similar technological characteristics
- Most common pathway for AI/ML medical devices
- Timeline: ~90 days average FDA review
- Evidence: Bench testing, sometimes clinical data; must show substantial equivalence to predicate
- Example: A new chest X-ray AI for pneumothorax detection can reference a previously cleared chest X-ray AI as its predicate

**De Novo Classification** — 'New type, low-to-moderate risk'
- Used when no predicate exists but the device is low-to-moderate risk
- Creates a new device classification that future 510(k)s can reference
- Timeline: ~150 days average FDA review
- Evidence: More clinical evidence than 510(k), performance data on representative populations
- Example: IDx-DR used the De Novo pathway because no autonomous AI diagnostic had been previously authorized

**Premarket Approval (PMA)** — 'High risk, requires clinical trials'
- Used for Class III devices (highest risk)
- Requires randomized clinical trials demonstrating safety and effectiveness
- Timeline: 6-12+ months FDA review
- Evidence: Pivotal clinical trials, manufacturing quality data, post-market surveillance plan
- Rare for AI/ML devices currently; most use 510(k) or De Novo

### Pathway Selection Decision Tree

1. Is there a predicate device with the same intended use?
   - **Yes:** 510(k) is likely appropriate
   - **No:** Go to step 2
2. Is the device high-risk (Class III)?
   - **Yes:** PMA required
   - **No:** De Novo is likely appropriate
3. Special considerations:
   - **Breakthrough Device designation:** For novel devices addressing unmet needs. Provides faster review and more FDA interaction.
   - **Clinical Decision Support (CDS) exemption:** Under the 21st Century Cures Act, certain CDS software is exempt from device regulation if it meets four criteria: (1) not intended to acquire/process/analyze medical images or signals, (2) displays or makes available the basis of its recommendations, (3) the HCP can independently review the basis, and (4) the HCP is not intended to primarily rely on the software.

### Real-World Examples

| Product | Pathway | Risk | Year |
|---|---|---|---|
| Viz.ai Stroke Triage | De Novo | II | 2018 |
| IDx-DR Diabetic Retinopathy | De Novo | II | 2018 |
| Aidoc Intracranial Hemorrhage | 510(k) | II | 2018 |
| Caption Health Cardiac Ultrasound | De Novo | II | 2020 |
| Paige Prostate Pathology | De Novo | II | 2021 |

**Key concept:** The CDS exemption under the 21st Century Cures Act is one of the most important regulatory boundaries in health AI. Understanding whether a product qualifies for this exemption determines whether it needs FDA review at all. The four criteria — especially the requirement that the HCP can 'independently review the basis' — are frequently tested in regulatory strategy discussions.

title: FDA's AI/ML-Specific Guidance
description: Predetermined Change Control Plans, Good Machine Learning Practice, and the Total Product Lifecycle approach.

learningObjectives.text: Explain the concept of a Predetermined Change Control Plan for AI/ML devices
List the principles of Good Machine Learning Practice (GMLP)
Describe how the Total Product Lifecycle approach applies to continuously learning AI

contentSections.body:
### The Challenge of Evolving AI

Traditional medical device regulation assumes a fixed product: you submit evidence, get approval, and the device does not change. AI/ML models, however, are designed to improve over time as they encounter new data. A locked model that cannot be updated becomes less accurate as clinical practice evolves.

The FDA recognized this tension and proposed the **Total Product Lifecycle (TPLC) approach** for AI/ML-based SaMD:

### Predetermined Change Control Plan (PCCP)

A PCCP allows manufacturers to pre-specify how their AI model will change after authorization. The plan includes:

- **Description of modifications:** What types of changes are anticipated (retraining on new data, threshold adjustment, feature addition)?
- **Modification protocol:** How will changes be developed, tested, and validated?
- **Performance benchmarks:** What metrics must the modified model meet before deployment?
- **Transparency:** How will users be notified of changes?

With an approved PCCP, manufacturers can update their AI models without submitting a new 510(k) or De Novo for each change — as long as the updates fall within the pre-specified plan. This was formalized in FDA guidance issued in 2023.

### Good Machine Learning Practice (GMLP)

In 2021, the FDA, Health Canada, and UK MHRA jointly published 10 guiding principles for GMLP:

1. Multi-disciplinary expertise is leveraged throughout the total product lifecycle
2. Good software engineering and security practices are implemented
3. Clinical study participants and datasets are representative of the intended patient population
4. Training datasets are independent of test datasets
5. Reference datasets are based on best available methods
6. Model design is tailored to the available data and intended use
7. Focus is placed on the performance of the human-AI team
8. Testing demonstrates device performance during clinically relevant conditions
9. Users are provided clear, essential information
10. Deployed models are monitored for performance and re-training risks are managed

These principles are not legally binding requirements but represent the regulatory agencies' expectations. Companies that align with GMLP are better positioned for successful regulatory submissions.

### Real-World Performance Monitoring

The FDA increasingly expects post-market surveillance plans for AI devices:
- How will real-world performance be measured?
- What triggers a safety investigation or model update?
- How will adverse events related to AI errors be reported?
- How will performance across demographic subgroups be monitored?

**Career tip:** Regulatory affairs roles in health AI are among the highest-paying positions in the field. 'Regulatory Affairs Specialist — AI/ML' and 'Digital Health Regulatory Strategy' roles require deep knowledge of FDA pathways, GMLP principles, and the ability to translate between engineering teams and regulatory agencies.

title: The EU AI Act and Health AI
description: How Europe's AI Act classifies and regulates healthcare AI systems.

learningObjectives.text: Describe the EU AI Act's risk-based classification system
Explain the requirements for high-risk AI systems under the EU AI Act
Compare the EU's approach to AI regulation with the US approach

contentSections.body:
### The EU AI Act: A Landmark Regulation

The EU AI Act, adopted in 2024, is the world's first comprehensive AI-specific regulation. Unlike the US sector-by-sector approach, the EU AI Act provides a horizontal framework that applies across all sectors, including healthcare.

### Risk-Based Classification

The EU AI Act classifies AI systems into four risk tiers:

**Unacceptable risk (banned):**
- Social scoring by governments
- Real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces (with limited exceptions)
- AI that exploits vulnerable populations
- Most healthcare AI does not fall here, but AI that manipulates patients into harmful decisions could qualify

**High risk:**
- AI used as a safety component of a medical device (SaMD)
- AI used in healthcare for diagnostic, treatment, or triage purposes
- **Most clinical AI falls into this category**

**Limited risk:**
- AI that interacts with humans (chatbots) must disclose that the user is interacting with AI
- Health chatbots and symptom checkers may fall here

**Minimal risk:**
- AI for administrative tasks, research without clinical impact
- No specific requirements beyond voluntary codes of conduct

### Requirements for High-Risk AI Systems

Healthcare AI classified as high-risk must comply with extensive requirements:

**Risk management system:** Comprehensive lifecycle risk management, including identification and mitigation of risks to health, safety, and fundamental rights.

**Data governance:** Training, validation, and testing datasets must be relevant, representative, free of errors, and complete. Specific attention to bias in data.

**Technical documentation:** Detailed documentation of the AI system's design, development, testing, and performance — more extensive than current FDA requirements.

**Transparency:** Users must receive clear information about the system's capabilities, limitations, and intended use.

**Human oversight:** High-risk AI must be designed to allow effective human oversight. Clinicians must be able to understand outputs, override decisions, and intervene.

**Accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity:** The system must achieve appropriate levels of accuracy, be resilient to errors, and be protected against manipulation.

### EU vs US Comparison

| Dimension | EU AI Act | US (FDA) |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Horizontal (all sectors) | Sector-specific (medical devices) |
| Focus | Fundamental rights + safety | Safety + effectiveness |
| Bias requirements | Explicit, detailed | Emerging in guidance |
| Transparency | Mandatory explanations | Encouraged (GMLP) |
| Penalties | Up to 7% of global turnover | Warning letters, recalls |
| Timeline | Phased: 2025-2027 | Ongoing |

**Career tip:** Health AI companies serving global markets must comply with both FDA and EU AI Act requirements. 'Global Regulatory Affairs' roles that span US and EU frameworks are increasingly in demand. Understanding both regulatory philosophies — the FDA's product-focused approach and the EU's rights-focused approach — is a major differentiator.

title: UK, Canada, and Global Regulatory Approaches
description: How the UK MHRA, Health Canada, and other regulators approach healthcare AI oversight.

learningObjectives.text: Describe the UK MHRA's approach to AI medical device regulation
Compare Health Canada's approach with FDA and EU approaches
Identify emerging international harmonization efforts for health AI regulation

contentSections.body:
### UK MHRA

Post-Brexit, the UK's Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) is developing its own AI regulatory framework:

- **Software and AI as a Medical Device Change Programme:** A modernization initiative to update medical device regulations for AI
- **GMLP co-authorship:** The MHRA co-authored the Good Machine Learning Practice principles with the FDA and Health Canada
- **Regulatory sandbox:** The MHRA operates an AI Airlock regulatory sandbox where companies can test AI devices in controlled real-world settings before formal submission
- **Adaptive licensing:** Exploring conditional approvals that allow AI devices to enter the market with initial evidence and generate real-world evidence during use

The UK aims to be more agile than the EU while maintaining safety standards — positioning itself as an attractive market for health AI companies.

### Health Canada

- **Pre-market pathway:** Similar to FDA (Class II, III, IV devices require licensing)
- **GMLP co-authorship:** Joint signatory with FDA and MHRA
- **Transparency:** Requires public listing of authorized AI medical devices
- **Post-market surveillance:** Active monitoring program for AI device performance
- **Notable difference:** Health Canada's review timelines are often shorter than FDA's, making it an attractive first-market-entry option

### Other Key Regulators

**Japan (PMDA):** Has approved AI medical devices through its existing pharmaceutical affairs law. Notable for approving an AI-powered endoscopy system (EndoBRAIN) for colorectal cancer detection.

**China (NMPA):** Rapidly growing AI medical device market. The NMPA has approved dozens of AI devices, particularly in imaging. Requires clinical trials conducted in Chinese populations.

**Australia (TGA):** Regulates AI software through its medical device framework. Generally aligned with international standards.

**Singapore (HSA):** Proactive regulatory sandbox approach. Published an AI governance framework with specific healthcare guidance.

### International Harmonization Efforts

- **IMDRF:** The International Medical Device Regulators Forum develops harmonized guidance that member regulators (FDA, EU, Health Canada, MHRA, Japan, China, Australia) reference
- **WHO guidance:** The WHO published 'Ethics and Governance of AI for Health' (2021) with six principles: protect autonomy, promote well-being, ensure transparency, foster responsibility, ensure inclusiveness, promote responsive and sustainable AI
- **G7 Hiroshima Process:** G7 nations agreed on voluntary AI principles that include healthcare-specific considerations

Despite harmonization efforts, significant differences remain. A device approved in the US may not be automatically accepted in the EU or China. Global regulatory strategy must account for each market's specific requirements.

**Key concept:** There is no single global regulatory pathway for health AI. Companies targeting international markets must develop market-specific regulatory strategies. However, alignment with GMLP principles and IMDRF guidance provides a strong foundation for submissions across multiple jurisdictions.

title: Liability and Legal Frameworks for Clinical AI
description: Who is responsible when AI makes a clinical error? Product liability, malpractice, and the evolving legal landscape.

learningObjectives.text: Explain the legal liability frameworks that apply to clinical AI errors
Distinguish between product liability (manufacturer) and malpractice (clinician) in AI-related harm
Describe how informed consent applies to AI-assisted clinical decisions

contentSections.body:
### The Liability Gap

When a clinical AI system contributes to patient harm, the legal question is: who is responsible?

**Product liability (manufacturer):**
- The AI manufacturer may be liable under product liability law if the AI had a design defect, manufacturing defect, or failure to warn
- **Design defect:** The AI was trained on biased data, producing systematically inaccurate results for certain populations
- **Manufacturing defect:** A software bug caused incorrect predictions in a specific deployment
- **Failure to warn:** The manufacturer did not adequately disclose the AI's limitations, accuracy rates, or intended use population

**Medical malpractice (clinician/institution):**
- Clinicians who rely on AI recommendations remain legally responsible for clinical decisions
- The standard of care question: is it malpractice to NOT use AI when it is available and improves outcomes? Is it malpractice to follow an incorrect AI recommendation without independent clinical judgment?
- Institutions may be liable for deploying AI without adequate validation, training, or oversight

**Shared liability:** In practice, liability is likely shared between manufacturer and clinician/institution, with the allocation depending on:
- How the AI was marketed (autonomous decision vs decision support)
- Whether the clinician had the ability and training to override
- Whether the institution followed reasonable validation and governance processes

### Informed Consent for AI

Should patients be informed when AI is involved in their care? The legal and ethical consensus is evolving:

- **The case for disclosure:** Patient autonomy requires knowing what tools are used in their care. The EU AI Act requires transparency about AI use in high-risk settings. The AMA has stated that patients should be informed about AI's role in their care.
- **The case against mandatory case-by-case consent:** If AI is validated, FDA-authorized, and used within its intended scope, requiring individual consent for every AI-assisted decision could be impractical and might create anxiety without improving outcomes.
- **Emerging practice:** Many health systems include AI disclosure in general treatment consent forms rather than requiring separate consent for each AI tool.

### Emerging Legal Trends

- **AI-specific legislation:** Several US states have proposed bills requiring disclosure of AI use in healthcare decisions
- **Tort liability for algorithms:** Legal scholarship is developing frameworks for holding algorithm developers liable under negligence or strict liability theories
- **Insurance implications:** Medical malpractice insurers are beginning to address AI in coverage policies, and some are requiring AI governance programs as a condition of coverage
- **Documentation requirements:** Courts may expect clinicians to document why they agreed with or overrode an AI recommendation, creating new documentation burdens

**Career tip:** The intersection of AI, healthcare, and law is a rapidly growing field. 'Health AI Policy Analyst,' 'Digital Health Legal Counsel,' and 'AI Risk Manager' roles are emerging at health systems, law firms, insurers, and AI companies. A background in health informatics combined with policy or legal knowledge is extremely valuable.

title: Classifying Your AI Product: A Practical Guide
description: How to determine whether your healthcare AI product requires FDA authorization and which pathway to pursue.

learningObjectives.text: Apply SaMD classification criteria to a novel healthcare AI product
Determine the appropriate FDA regulatory pathway for a given clinical AI system
Identify when the CDS exemption applies and when it does not

contentSections.body:
### Step 1: Is It a Medical Device?

Apply the FDA's definition: does the software have an intended use that is medical? Consider:

**Likely a medical device:**
- Software that analyzes medical images and provides a diagnosis or detection result
- Software that predicts clinical deterioration and recommends intervention
- Software that determines treatment dosing based on patient-specific parameters
- Software that triages patients by clinical acuity

**Likely NOT a medical device:**
- Administrative software (scheduling, billing)
- General wellness apps (fitness tracking, meditation, nutrition tracking without disease-specific claims)
- Clinical communication tools (secure messaging, telehealth video)
- EHR software (unless it includes SaMD-qualifying CDS features)

**Gray area (CDS exemption check):**
If the software provides clinical decision support, apply the four Cures Act criteria:
1. Not intended to acquire, process, or analyze a medical image or signal from an in vitro diagnostic device or a pattern/signal from a signal acquisition system
2. Intended for the purpose of displaying, analyzing, or printing medical information about a patient or other medical information
3. Intended for the purpose of supporting or providing recommendations to an HCP about prevention, diagnosis, or treatment
4. Intended for the purpose of enabling the HCP to independently review the basis for the recommendation

All four must be met for the exemption. If the AI is intended for non-HCP users (patients), or if the HCP cannot independently review the basis, the exemption does not apply.

### Step 2: Determine the Risk Classification

Using the IMDRF framework:
- Map your product to a significance level (inform, drive, treat/diagnose)
- Map to a healthcare situation seriousness level (non-serious, serious, critical)
- The combination determines the risk category (I-IV)

### Step 3: Choose the Pathway

**Search for predicates:** Use the FDA's 510(k) database and AI/ML device list to find previously authorized devices with similar intended use.
- Found a predicate: 510(k)
- No predicate, low-moderate risk: De Novo
- High risk (Class III): PMA

### Step 4: Pre-Submission Meeting

The FDA offers **pre-submission (Pre-Sub)** meetings where companies can discuss their regulatory strategy before formal submission. This is strongly recommended for novel AI products. The meeting allows you to:
- Confirm device classification
- Discuss appropriate performance testing
- Align on clinical evidence expectations
- Discuss the PCCP if adaptive learning is planned

### Common Pitfalls

- **Marketing creep:** A product marketed for 'wellness' that adds diagnostic language enters SaMD territory
- **Intended use drift:** A product cleared for one clinical indication being promoted for another
- **Under-classification:** Assuming a De Novo product is exempt from regulation based on a misapplication of the CDS criteria

**Career tip:** Regulatory strategy consulting for health AI startups is a lucrative and growing field. Many startups lack regulatory expertise and hire consultants to navigate classification, pathway selection, and submission preparation. Experience with actual FDA submissions is the key differentiator.

title: Preparing a Regulatory Submission for AI/ML Devices
description: What goes into an FDA submission for a clinical AI product: performance testing, clinical evidence, and documentation.

learningObjectives.text: Describe the key components of an FDA submission for an AI/ML medical device
Apply performance testing design principles to an AI/ML submission
Identify the clinical evidence requirements for different submission types

contentSections.body:
### Submission Components

A typical 510(k) or De Novo submission for an AI/ML device includes:

**Device description:**
- Intended use and indications for use
- Detailed algorithm description (architecture, training approach, feature inputs)
- User interface description
- Hardware and software requirements

**Performance testing:**
- **Standalone performance:** Model accuracy metrics (AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV) on a held-out test set that was not used during development
- **Subgroup analysis:** Performance broken down by age, sex, race/ethnicity, disease severity, and imaging equipment (for imaging AI)
- **Comparison to predicate** (for 510(k)): Demonstrate substantial equivalence in performance
- **Reader studies** (for assistive AI): Compare clinician performance with and without AI assistance

**Training data description:**
- Sources, size, demographics
- Labeling methodology and inter-rater agreement
- Data preprocessing and augmentation steps
- Train/validation/test split methodology

**Clinical evidence:**
- For 510(k): May be bench testing only if predicate has established clinical evidence
- For De Novo: Typically requires clinical performance data, often from a prospective study
- For PMA: Pivotal clinical trial data

### Performance Testing Design

**Test dataset requirements:**
- Must be independent from training and validation data (no overlap)
- Must be representative of the intended use population
- Ground truth labels must be established by a rigorous methodology (expert consensus, biopsy confirmation, clinical outcomes)
- Dataset size must be sufficient to demonstrate statistically significant performance claims

**Study design for reader studies:**
In a reader study, clinicians interpret cases with and without AI assistance:
- Multi-reader, multi-case (MRMC) design is the standard
- Readers are representative of the intended user population (e.g., general radiologists, not only subspecialists)
- Cases are enriched for the target condition (otherwise too few positive cases)
- Crossover design: each reader reads each case twice (once with AI, once without, with a washout period)

**Reporting standards:**
- Follow STARD (Standards for Reporting Diagnostic Accuracy) guidelines
- Report confidence intervals, not just point estimates
- Include failure analysis for misclassified cases
- Provide calibration assessment if the model outputs probabilities

### Post-Submission Process

After submission, FDA reviewers may request additional information (AI/ML-specific questions typically focus on training data representativeness, model robustness, and software lifecycle management). Typical review timelines: 90-120 days for 510(k), 150-300 days for De Novo.

**Key concept:** The FDA reviews not just the model's performance but the entire lifecycle: how it was built, how it was tested, how it will be maintained, and how it will be monitored after deployment. A high-performing model with poor documentation, non-representative testing, or no post-market monitoring plan will face significant review challenges.

title: Post-Market Surveillance and Compliance
description: Maintaining regulatory compliance after authorization: adverse event reporting, quality systems, and real-world monitoring.

learningObjectives.text: Describe post-market surveillance requirements for FDA-authorized AI devices
Apply quality management system principles to AI/ML device lifecycle management
Design a post-market monitoring plan for a deployed clinical AI system

contentSections.body:
### Post-Market Obligations

FDA authorization is not the end of the regulatory process. Manufacturers have ongoing obligations:

**Medical Device Reporting (MDR):**
- Mandatory reporting of adverse events to the FDA within 30 days (or 5 days for events requiring remedial action)
- For AI devices, reportable events include: AI-driven misdiagnosis that led to patient harm, AI system failure during critical clinical use, AI providing incorrect treatment recommendations

**Recalls and corrections:**
- If a safety issue is identified, the manufacturer must initiate a recall or correction
- The FDA classifies recalls by severity: Class I (most serious, reasonable probability of death), Class II (temporary or reversible health consequences), Class III (unlikely to cause adverse health consequences)
- AI-specific recall scenarios: training data contamination discovered post-deployment, model drift causing systematic performance degradation, cybersecurity vulnerability enabling model manipulation

**Quality System Regulation (QSR) / ISO 13485:**
- Manufacturers must maintain a quality management system covering design controls, document controls, production controls, and corrective/preventive actions (CAPA)
- For AI devices, this includes version control for models, training data provenance tracking, and change management for model updates

### Designing a Post-Market Monitoring Plan

A comprehensive monitoring plan for clinical AI includes:

**Performance monitoring:**
- Track real-world AUROC, sensitivity, specificity against deployment benchmarks
- Stratify by demographic subgroups to detect emerging disparities
- Monitor calibration drift (are predicted probabilities still accurate?)

**Data drift monitoring:**
- Statistical tests for input feature distribution shifts
- Monitor for new data patterns not present in training data
- Track EHR system changes that may affect data format or content

**User feedback collection:**
- Structured clinician feedback on AI accuracy and usability
- Override reason tracking and analysis
- Reporting pathway for suspected AI errors

**Outcome tracking:**
- Compare patient outcomes for AI-assisted vs non-AI-assisted decisions (where possible)
- Track for unintended consequences (over-testing, alert fatigue, workflow disruption)

**Retraining governance:**
- Define triggers for model retraining (performance below threshold, significant data drift, new clinical evidence)
- If operating under a PCCP, ensure retraining stays within pre-specified parameters
- If changes exceed PCCP scope, prepare a new regulatory submission

**Documentation:**
- Maintain a complete audit trail of model versions, performance metrics, retraining events, and clinical feedback
- This documentation is essential for regulatory inspections and liability defense

**Career tip:** Post-market surveillance for AI devices is a rapidly growing function. 'AI Quality Engineer,' 'Post-Market Surveillance Analyst,' and 'Clinical AI Monitoring Specialist' roles combine quality systems expertise with ML monitoring skills. These roles are critical as the number of deployed clinical AI devices continues to grow.

title: LLMs and Foundation Models in Healthcare: FDA & EU AI Act
description: Large language models and foundation models present novel regulatory challenges. Current FDA guidance, EU AI Act GPAI provisions, and what clinical AI governance teams need to know.

learningObjectives.text: Describe how LLMs differ from traditional SaMD in FDA's regulatory framework
Identify the EU AI Act provisions relevant to General Purpose AI (GPAI) models
Apply FDA's proposed marketing submission pathway for AI-enabled devices to an LLM use case
Evaluate when an LLM in healthcare requires 510(k) vs De Novo vs PMA

contentSections.body:
### Why LLMs Challenge Existing Frameworks

Traditional FDA medical device regulation was designed for deterministic software. LLMs are: non-deterministic (same input can produce different outputs), continuously updated via RLHF/fine-tuning, context-sensitive in ways that are difficult to pre-specify, and capable of performing multiple functions simultaneously. The FDA's 2021 AI/ML SaMD Action Plan focused on predetermined change control plans (PCCPs) for adaptive algorithms — but PCCPs assume bounded adaptation within a known architecture. Foundation models trained on internet-scale data are a different paradigm entirely.

### FDA's Current Approach

As of 2025, FDA has NOT issued LLM-specific guidance. FDA applies the existing SaMD framework: if an LLM is 'intended to diagnose, cure, mitigate, treat, or prevent disease,' it is a medical device subject to 510(k), De Novo, or PMA review.

**Clinical use cases that likely require marketing authorization:**
- AI that diagnoses conditions from clinical notes
- LLMs that generate treatment recommendations
- AI triage tools in ED settings

**Lower-risk uses that may qualify for exemptions:**
- Administrative uses (prior auth letters, clinical documentation assistance that requires clinician review)
- Clinical education tools
- Coding assistance

The FDA's Digital Health Center of Excellence is developing LLM-specific guidance — watch for updates at fda.gov/medical-devices.

### EU AI Act — General Purpose AI (GPAI)

Articles 51-56 of the EU AI Act (fully applicable from August 2026) create special rules for GPAI models — models trained on broad data and capable of performing many tasks (this includes GPT-4, Gemini, Claude, Llama). GPAI model providers must: maintain technical documentation, comply with EU copyright law for training data, and publish summaries of training data.

GPAI models with 'systemic risk' (those exceeding 10^25 FLOPs in training compute) face additional obligations: adversarial testing, serious incident reporting to EU AI Office, and cybersecurity measures.

For healthcare deployment of GPAI: the EU AI Act classifies most clinical AI as 'high-risk' (Annex III), requiring conformity assessments, CE marking equivalent, and post-market monitoring.

### Practical Governance Implications

Health systems deploying LLMs need:

1. A 'fit for purpose' assessment — what is the intended use and does it trigger FDA/EU oversight?
2. A vendor AI governance questionnaire — ask about FDA status, training data, bias testing, GPAI classification.
3. A clinical AI governance committee review for any LLM touching clinical decisions.
4. Transparency requirements — clinicians and patients should know when AI is involved.
5. Human-in-the-loop requirements — the most defensible deployment is AI as a decision support tool, never autonomous.
6. Audit logging — all LLM interactions in clinical workflows should be logged for review.

**Career tip:** Clinical AI governance is one of the fastest-growing health IT roles. Hospitals are hiring Clinical AI Governance Leads, AI Ethicists, and AI Safety Officers. Skills needed: FDA regulatory knowledge, clinical workflow understanding, fairness/bias assessment, vendor contract negotiation. The American Medical Association (AMA) has published a framework for clinical AI oversight that is widely referenced by health system governance committees.

title: Prospective Evaluation of Clinical AI: CONSORT-AI & SPIRIT-AI
description: How to properly evaluate clinical AI tools before deployment: reporting standards, trial designs, and what rigorous evidence looks like.

learningObjectives.text: Apply CONSORT-AI and SPIRIT-AI reporting standards to an AI trial
Distinguish between RCT, stepped-wedge, and pre/post designs for clinical AI evaluation
Identify the key elements of a clinical AI validation study

contentSections.body:
### Why Prospective Evaluation Matters

Most clinical AI is evaluated using retrospective data. Retrospective validation cannot capture: alert fatigue effects, workflow integration challenges, how clinicians interact with AI outputs (automation bias), or population drift over time. Prospective trials answer the real question: 'Does deploying this AI in clinical practice improve outcomes?' The evidence bar for AI is rising. The BMJ and NEJM increasingly require CONSORT-AI adherence for AI trial publication.

### CONSORT-AI and SPIRIT-AI

**CONSORT-AI** (Consolidated Standards of Reporting Trials — AI extension, 2020): extends the standard CONSORT checklist with AI-specific items. Key additions:
- Describe training data and algorithm details
- Report participant flow for AI model as well as patients
- Describe how AI outputs were communicated to clinicians
- Report adherence to AI recommendations
- Identify subgroup analyses by demographic

**SPIRIT-AI** (Standard Protocol Items: Recommendations for Interventional Trials — AI extension): the pre-registration equivalent of CONSORT-AI. Used when registering an AI trial protocol. Specifies: the AI intervention version, update policy during the trial, human oversight plan.

### Trial Designs for Clinical AI

**(1) Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT):** Gold standard. Patients (or clinicians) randomized to AI-assisted vs. standard care. Challenge: contamination when AI-assisted clinicians change their general practice.

**(2) Stepped-wedge design:** All sites eventually receive the AI, but the rollout is staggered randomly. This generates a comparison between sites that have vs. haven't yet received AI at any given time. Useful when withholding AI from a control group is ethically problematic.

**(3) Pre/post comparison:** Simplest, most susceptible to confounding from secular trends.

**(4) Cluster randomization:** Randomize by site (hospital unit, clinic) rather than individual patient — avoids contamination, better matches how AI is deployed.

**Career tip:** If you are evaluating an AI vendor's evidence claims, ask: Was it prospective or retrospective? Did it measure clinical outcomes (mortality, readmissions) or surrogate metrics (AUC, sensitivity)? Was it conducted at the same type of institution as yours? Did it account for workflow effects? These questions distinguish sophisticated AI governance from checkbox compliance.
